preFix.title: Hazelnut meringue roulade
preFix.subtitle: A soft meringue roll filled with cream and raspberries
preFix.excerpt: Hazelnut meringue roulade is made from a flat sheet of nut meringue baked until just set, then rolled around whipped cream and raspberries. It cracks as it rolls: this is expected and part of the look. The exterior is crisp in places, the interior chewy, and the hazelnuts give the meringue depth beyond a plain white roll.
preFix.sourceNotes: Meringue roulades became a fixture of British dinner party and celebration baking from the 1970s onward, popularised by cookery writers including Delia Smith and Nigella Lawson. The hazelnut version is a natural development from the plain meringue roll, with the ground nuts adding structure to what is otherwise a very soft, collapsing sheet. The combination of hazelnuts, cream, and raspberries follows a standard British summer party formula.

content.text: The meringue is made by the standard method: whisk egg whites to stiff peaks, add sugar gradually, then fold in the ground hazelnuts. Unlike a classic meringue, this one is only partially dried out in the oven so it stays pliable enough to roll without shattering. It will crack visibly as you roll it; this is correct and part of the finished look rather than a failure.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Line a 33 x 23 cm tin with baking paper, leaving an overhang on all sides. Whisk 4 egg whites with a hand mixer in a large, clean bowl until they hold stiff peaks. Add 200 g caster sugar a tablespoon at a time, whisking between each addition, until the meringue is thick, glossy, and the sugar has dissolved (rub a little between your fingers: no graininess). Fold in 75 g finely ground toasted hazelnuts with a large metal spoon. Spread evenly into the prepared tin. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the surface is set and barely coloured. The meringue should feel firm to a light press but give slightly.

Lay a clean sheet of baking paper on the work surface and dust with icing sugar. Turn the meringue out onto it. Peel away the lining paper. Leave to cool completely; 30 minutes. Spread the whipped cream evenly over the surface, leaving a 2 cm border at one short edge. Scatter raspberries over the cream. Using the paper as a guide, roll from the short end without the border, pulling the paper away as you go. Transfer to a serving plate with the join underneath. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour before slicing.

items.problem: The meringue shattered rather than cracking neatly
items.solution: The meringue was overbaked and too dry. It should feel set but still slightly soft in the centre when you take it from the oven. Reduce the baking time by 3 to 5 minutes.
The cream was whipped to peaks that were too stiff, or there was too much filling. Whip to soft, just-holding peaks and keep the layer no thicker than 1 cm.
